coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots obtained from cellulose and their applications as sensors for metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Passos Zattar, Alessandra Pires
Paulo de Mesquita, João
Pereira, Fabiano Vargas
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) can be obtained from different sources including wastes and green biomass materials, which, together with the method of preparation, can contribute to reduce the cost of production of these nanomaterials. In this work we have prepared different CDs from cellulose and using hydrothermal carbonization. The obtained nanocarbons were functionalized to enhance the quantum yield (QY) by two different methods: in a one pot reaction where the CDs were obtained in the presence of certain amount of ammonia or through a reaction with a oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) diamine (PEG1500 N) after the preparation of CDs. The obtained CDs were characterized by different techniques including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV-VIS spectroscopy, Fluorescence, and Potentiometric Titration. Size distribution measurements from isolated nanoparticles showed that the different obtained CDs presented a narrow size distribution, with the diameters in the range 0.7–3.0 nm. The CDs which presented high values of QY were used as probes for different metal ions, showing considerable selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ ions and a limit of detection of 0.15 μM in the case of CDs modified with PEG1500 N oligomers. Finally, the results presented here show the possibility to obtain CDs with a relatively low cost method, using only water as solvent and with potential applications in different areas including those as probe for metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Sensor
Carbon nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modified carbon dots with ammonium hydroxide
Modified carbon dots with PEG1500 N
Quenching effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs
Characterization of the unmodified carbon dots
Characterization of the modified carbon dots
Modified CDs as selective probes for different metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank CNPq (EDT Universal Nº 28/2018) and Fapemig (EDT Universal N° 1/2017) for financial support. Centro de Microscopia-UFMG is also gratefully acknowledged for providing the images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of the cellulose (CEL) and the carbon dots (CDCEL).
TEM (a) and HRTEM images of the CDCEL sample.

UV-VIS absorption and fluorescence spectra obtained at different wavelength of excitation for CDCEL sample. The inset shows the carbon dots sample illuminated under natural (left) and UV (right) light source.
FTIR spectra of the CDCEL, CDCELN and CDCELPEGN samples.
TEM and HRTEM images of the CDCELN (a, b) and CDCELPEGN (c, d) samples.

UV-VIS absorption and fluorescence spectra obtained at different wavelength of excitation for CDCELN sample. The inset shows the carbon dots sample illuminated under natural (left) and UV (right) light source.

UV-VIS absorption and fluorescence spectra obtained at different wavelength of excitation for CDCELPEGN sample. The inset shows the carbon dots sample illuminated under natural (left) and UV (right) light source.
Photoluminescence intensity of the neat CDCELN (a) and CDCELPEGN (b) compared with the intensities after the addition of the different metal ions.
Stern-Volmer plots of CDCELN (a) and CDCELPEGN (b) photoluminescence intensity quenching by Fe3+.
Schematic diagram for the preparation of the obtained CDs.

a) Schematic representation of the reaction to form amide and nitrile in the presence of ammonia from an oxygenated functional group. b) reactions to form nitriles from the amination of phenol groups and subsequent oxidation to form nitriles.
Schematic representation of the CDCEL sample functionalization with PEG1500 N.
Schematic representation of fluorescence quenching mechanism of the obtained CDs with Fe3+ ions.
Results obtained by fitting experimental titration curve of the CDCEL sample.
Results obtained by fitting experimental titration curve of the CDCELN sample.

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon dots obtained from cellulose and their applications as sensors for metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry – Universidade Federal de Minas Geras, Av. Antônio Carlos, 6627, Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, MG, CEP 31270-901, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry – Universidade Federal de Minas Geras

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry - Universidade Federal de Minas Geras, Av. Antonio Carlos, 6627, Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, MG, CEP 31270-901

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry - Universidade Federal dos Vales do Jequitinhonha e Mucuri, Rodovia MGT 367 - Km 583, nº 5000, Alto da Jacuba CEP, 39100-000, Diamantina, MG, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry - Universidade Federal dos Vales do Jequitinhonha e Mucuri

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry - Universidade Federal dos Vales do Jequitinhonha e Mucuri, Rodovia MGT 367 - Km 583, nº 5000, Alto da Jacuba CEP, 39100-000, Diamantina, MG, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) can be obtained from different sources including wastes and green biomass materials, which, together with the method of preparation, can contribute to reduce the cost of production of these nanomaterials.

In this work we have prepared different CDs from cellulose and using hydrothermal carbonization. The obtained nanocarbons were functionalized to enhance the quantum yield (QY) by two different methods: in a one pot reaction where the CDs were obtained in the presence of certain amount of ammonia or through a reaction with a oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) diamine (PEG1500 N) after the preparation of CDs. The obtained CDs were characterized by different techniques including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV-VIS spectroscopy, Fluorescence, and Potentiometric Titration. Size distribution measurements from isolated nanoparticles showed that the different obtained CDs presented a narrow size distribution, with the diameters in the range 0.7–3.0 nm. The CDs which presented high values of QY were used as probes for different metal ions, showing considerable selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ ions and a limit of detection of 0.15 μM in the case of CDs modified with PEG1500 N oligomers.

Finally, the results presented here show the possibility to obtain CDs with a relatively low cost method, using only water as solvent and with potential applications in different areas including those as probe for metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increased interest of many researchers in the last few years because they represent a relatively new carbon nanomaterial with interesting properties and therefore with various potential applications in different areas [1,2]. These nanoparticles are typically quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanomaterials, with diameters in the range of 2–10 nm and presenting interesting optical properties, such as strong absorption in the UV region, photoluminescence and fantastic photostability [2,3]. Also, CDs are non-toxic, form stable suspensions in aqueous media and present a chemically reactive surface, which allows a relatively easy functionalization [4]. All these characteristics make them very versatile with promising applications in various fields such as in the biomedical area (bioimaging and drug/gene delivery), energy (photocatalysis, supercapacitors and photovoltaic energy) and in optics (data security, photoluminescent markers, light emitting diodes and sensors for metal ions and other substances) [1].

Two main strategies are usually described to obtain CDs, generally classified as “top-down” and “bottom-up” preparation methods. The first one involves the preparation of carbon nanoparticles from larger carbon structures such as activated carbon [5], graphite oxide [6], or carbon nanotube [7] using for example electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation or arch discharge [7,8]. The bottom-up methods refer to the preparation of CDs from low molar precursors or macromolecules such as polymers or biomaterials using mainly hydrothermal carbonization, pyrolysis or microwave-assisted methods [9,10].

In the last few years, considering mainly hydrothermal carbonization method, some advances have been achieved in the surface passivation or heteroatom doping approaches to enhance que optical properties of CDs. Zhang et al. described the preparation of a dual-emissive N,S co-doped carbon with a long emission wavelength (λem = 648 nm). The CDs were prepared via simple hydrothermal treatment with a relatively high value of quantum yield (QY) and were used as an ultrasensitive Ag+ probe sensor [11]. Liu and co-workers developed a facile approach for the preparation of a barium-doped carbon dot by solvothermal method with yellow fluorescence emission and high value of QY. The obtained nanoparticles were used as fluorescent probes in the detection of trace water in organic solvents [12].

Comparing with the semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), which are prepared from heavy metals such as cadmium and selenium [2,13], the CDs represent a breakthrough in terms of fluorescent nanoparticles, not only because they are not toxic but also because they can be prepared through environmentally friendly methods and from low cost precursors, such as wastes and green biomass materials [14].

In this regard, cellulose, the most abundant polymer found in nature can represent an interesting precursor for the preparation of CDs since its use can reduce the final cost for obtaining these carbon nanoparticles. Also, the presence of large amount of oxygenated functional groups on this biopolymer (hydroxyls groups) can act as an advantage for the formation of carbon dots [4].

One important application of carbon dots is the possibility of use them as sensors for different substances including pollutants, heavy metal and radioactive ions [15–18]. Comparing with different detection techniques, including atomic absorption spectrometry, Auger-electron spectroscopy and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), fluorescence-based detection methods with photostable nanomaterials have advantages such as high sensitivity, great simplicity, short response time and low cost [14,19,20].

Heavy metal ions can be widely distributed in environmental systems and their detection and quantification is of crucial importance for ecosystems and human health. Among them, iron is an abundant essential element for living organisms and it plays an essential role in human body, participating in a variety of metabolic process. However, an excess of Fe3+, mainly due to water pollution can cause different biological disorders and diseases such as diabetes, dysfunction of certain organs and also neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's diseases [21,22]. Therefore, it is of pivotal importance to probe and monitor the presence and concentration of Fe3+ through a low cost, selective and fast technique [14].

In this work, carbon dots were obtained from cellulose through an environmentally friendly method, using only water as solvent. The obtained carbon nanoparticles were modified by two different methods to enhance the quantum yield (QY) and those CDs which presented higher QY values were used as probe for different metal ions, showing considerable selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ ions.

Cellulose kraft pulp was used as received as precursor of the carbon dots. Ammonium hydroxide solution (30%) was obtained from Neon (SP, Brazil), whereas poly(ethylene glycol) diamine (PEG1500 N) and thionyl chloride was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA).

The metal salts Fe(NO3)3.9H2O and AgNO3 were purchased from FMaia (São Paulo, Brazil), HgCl2 was obtained from Merck (USA), Cu(NO3)2 and Pb(NO3)2 were from Neon, CoCl2.6H2O was purchased from Vetec, FeSO4.7H2O from Sulfal and ZnSO4.7H2O was obtained from Synth (São Paulo, Brazil).

Firstly, 0.8 g of bleached cellulose fiber was placed in 40 mL of deionized water and transferred into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave which was sealed and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After the hydrothermal carbonization process, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the resulting product was centrifuged at 8200 rpm for 10 min. Then, the sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dried in a freeze-dryer. The CDs sample obtained through this procedure was named CDCEL.
The modified CDs were obtained by two different strategies: in a single step, during the hydrothermal carbonization process or in an additional step of functionalization after sample preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modified carbon dots with ammonium hydroxide
ce:section.ce:para: The CDCELN sample was prepared in the same way as described before for the unmodified CDs, adding 500 μL of ammonium hydroxide solution (30%) within the mixture in the autoclave. The CDs obtained through this procedure was named CDCELN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modified carbon dots with PEG1500 N
ce:section.ce:para: The CDCELPEGN sample was obtained through a reaction with oligomeric PEG1500 N as described elsewhere [23] with minor modifications.

Firstly, 25 mg of CDCEL previously oven dried was added to 5 mL of distilled thionyl chloride and refluxed for 6 h. Then, the thionyl chloride was removed using a rotaevaporator under reduced pressure, and 250 mg of dried PEG 1500 N was added to the reaction. The system was heated to 110 °C under reflux for 72 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Finally, the reaction was cooled and the modified carbon dots were dispersed in 20 ml of water and centrifuged to remove the precipitate (excess of unreacted PEG1500 N). The modified carbon nanoparticles prepared through this methodology was named CDCELPEGN.
Scheme 1 shows a schematic representation on the preparation of the different CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CDs with higher values of quantum yield (QY), CDCELN and CDCELPEGN, were studied as sensors for metal ions. In order to evaluate the selectivity, 25 μL of a solution of different metal ions (0.01 mol/L) were added to 2.5 mL of CD suspension (20 mg L−1). After 3 min of the metal ion addition to the CD suspension under magnetic stirring, the fluorescence intensity value was recorded.

In order to evaluate the sensitivity and calculate the limit of detection (LOD), different amounts (15, 30, 45, 60, 110, 150, 210, 250, 310 and 350 μL) of a 50 μM solution of the selected metal ion were added to 2.5 mL of a 20 mg mL−1 suspension of CDs and the fluorescence intensity was recorded after 3 min to equilibrate.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs were taken using a Tecnai G2-20 SuperTwin FEI with 200 kV acceleration voltage. The aqueous CDs suspensions were deposited on a carbon film TEM grid.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a PerkinElmer Spectrum RXI in a wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. The samples were prepared using the KBr disk method.

UV-VIS spectroscopy measurements were made using a Varian Cary100 Spectrophotometer. The carbon dots samples were diluted and adequate volumes of the diluted solutions were placed in quartz cuvettes with 1 cm of optical path to obtain the spectra. The fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer.

The quantum yield (QY) was calculated using quinine sulfate as reference (QY of 54% when excited at 340 nm) and the following equation:
(Equation 1)
∅
C D
=
∅
S T
(
S
C D

S T

η
C D
2
η
S T
2
)
Where ST is the standard (sulfate quinine), CD is the carbon dots sample, Φ
CD is the quantum yield of the CDs, S is the angular coefficient and η is the refractive index. The sulfate quinine solution was prepared using 0.1 M H2SO4 whereas deionized water was used to prepare the carbon dots suspensions. The refractive index of the sulfate quinine solution and the CDs suspensions are ∼1.33.

Potentiometric titration curves were obtained at 25 °C using a SCHOTT automatic titrator (Titroline 7000). The detailed protocol describing the calibration of the electrode and the computational algorithm used to fit the experimental data is described elsewhere [24]. A CO2-free NaOH solution was the titrant of the sample containing 10 mg of the CD in an HCl solution in an electrochemical cell. For the titration, around 10 mg of the sample was dispersed in an HCl solution (0,0036 mol L−1) in an electrochemical cell and titrated with a CO2-free NaOH solution (0.0134 mol L−1). The acid-base potentiometric titration curves were adjusted using a non-linear method and the amount of acid groups on the surface of the CDs were determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the unmodified carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of different functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots plays an important role on the characteristics of the carbon nanoparticles such as solubility and optical properties [2,4]. The functional groups were characterized using FTIR and potentiometric titration. Fig. 1 shows the FTIR spectra of the precursor cellulose and the carbon dots.

The spectrum of cellulose showed typical absorptions of cellulose I [25]. The band at 3385 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration. The stretching vibrations of the C–H groups were shown at 2890 cm−1 whereas the –CH2 bending is centered at 1443 cm−1. The presence of vibrations related to the saccharide structure were found between 1170 and 970 cm−1 and centered at 1057 cm−1 [22,26].

The spectrum of the CDCEL shows the weakening of the –CH vibration bands at 2890 cm−1, evidencing a significant degradation of the saccharide structure [27]. Also, the bending vibrations of the –CH2 group, at 1443 cm−1 present in the cellulose, were not observed in this sample. The band at 3155 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration on the surface of the carbon structures. In the region between 1550 and 1800 cm−1 it can be verified the presence of 4 absorptions at 1596, 1670, ∼1720 and 1774 cm−1 attributed to different functional groups, CC of aromatic structures and CO of amides, carboxylics and esters [28]. The signals at 1136 and 1075 cm−1 are due to stretching vibrations of C–O–C whereas the band at 1596 cm−1 can be attributed to CC stretching vibration [25].

The possible process of forming CDs from a complex macromolecular structure such as cellulose can be described as following. The cellulose chains are hydrolyzed to glucose, which is then degraded to form organic acids (such as formic or malonic acids) or hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) [29]. Then, this process is followed by the polymerization, forming polyfuranes and carbonization of the structures via intramolecular dehydration, forming the carbon nanoparticles [26].

The great diversity of functional groups presents on the CDs surfaces, which can exhibit different acidity constants, were also characterized by potentiometric titration. The concentration of the functional groups present on the CDs determines the acidic or basic character of the carbon nanomaterial and the pKa values depend on the neighborhood of the functional group and the size of the sp2 structures [21].

Fig. S1 shows the potentiometric titration curve for the CDCEL sample and Table 1 shows the correspondence between pKa values and the functional groups concentration found for the sample CDCEL. Among the most common functional groups found in carbon dots are surface carboxylics with pKa< 5, lactones with pKa between 6 and 8, and surface phenols with pKa> 8 [30]. The results obtained by fitting experimental titration curve showed that the CDCEL sample presents a large number of phenolic groups, 69.9%, 21.4% of carboxylic acid groups and 8.7% of lactones.

Fig. 2 a and b shows transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of the CDCEL sample. The image was obtained from a dilute aqueous suspension (∼0.1% wt/v) of the carbon dots. The CDCEL suspensions were relatively well dispersed, with a zeta potential value of −19.8 mV, in an aqueous medium at pH ∼6.5. This value of zeta potential reveals that some aggregates of CDs may be present even in the suspension, and mainly after the water evaporation to prepare the sample to TEM measurements.

The TEM image of the CDCEL sample (Fig. 2a) reveals some isolated particles with a quasi-spherical morphology and also some agglomerates. Some isolated CDs and also some aggregates are marked with red circles to aid visualization due to the low contrast of the nanoparticles against the carbon film used as substrate. The clusters of CDs are expected due to strong hydrogen bond interactions that are present between the nanoparticles, mainly due to the large amount of hydroxyl (from carboxylic and phenolic groups).

The size distribution measurements of isolated nanoparticles obtained from several TEM images were determined to be in the range of 0.8–2.6 nm with an average size around 1.5 nm. The size distribution histogram with a Gaussian fitting curve for the CDCEL sample is shown in Fig. S2, in the Supplementary Material.
HRTEM image (Fig. 2b) shows the crystalline structure of the obtained CDs revealing an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite.

Fig. 3 shows UV-VIS absorption and the photoluminescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths for the CDCEL sample dispersed in water. The inset shows the CD suspension under natural light and also irradiated with UV light. The CDCEL sample presented a transparent brown yellow color aspect when illuminated with natural light and a relatively weak luminescence in the blue region of spectrum when irradiated with a UV light source.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum presented an intense optical absorption band at 250 nm assigned to aromatic π-π* transitions of the conjugated CC [4]. It was also possible to observe a low-intensity broad band around 330–350 nm due to n → π * transitions of CO of different functional groups. The n → π * transition band for this sample present a much lower intensity (comparing to π-π* transition) possibly due to the large amount of phenol groups which increase the quantity of π-π* transitions.

The photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 3) of the CDCEL sample show the peak emissions maxima between 430 and 460 nm as a function of the excitation wavelength (from 260 to 400 nm). The maximum intensity of the peak emission shift to higher wavelengths as a function of the excitation wavelength. This behavior is normally observed in CDs due to a certain number of surface defects present on the nanoparticles. Also, the excitation dependence has been described as a result of optical selection of CDs with different sizes (quantum effect) [31]. Since the size distribution of the CDs obtained here were relatively monodisperse, we believe that the excitation dependence is mainly due to the surface functional groups found on CDs with distinct energy levels [2]. This behavior highlights the importance of the presence of different functional groups and the functionalization of CDs that can significantly change the optical properties of carbon nanoparticles for different potential applications.

The measurement of photoluminescence intensity as a function of time for CDCEL was also investigated. Time evolution of the PL intensity upon excitation at 340 nm is shown in Fig. S3a and indicated this sample did not suffer photobleaching behavior in the period of time investigated.

The calculated fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for this sample using the slope method and quinine sulfate as the reference standard [32] was calculated to be ∼1.0%. Fig. S4 shows the photoluminescence intensity and absorbance of the CDCEL sample and the standard quinine sulfate, both excited at 340 nm.

For many carbon dots applications, it is relevant to have a relatively high quantum yield. With the aim to improve the optical properties, mainly with regard to the quantum yield, the chemical modification of the CDs surface was carried out using different strategies, and the results are described in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the modified carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to modify the CDs surface to enhance the quantum yield of the nanoparticles, firstly, a simple strategy was used, which allowed the preparation of modified CDs in a one-step procedure, through the preparation of the CDs in the presence of a certain amount of ammonia for the functionalization of the nanoparticles with groups containing nitrogen. The increase of quantum yield of the CDs can be associated to the creation of energy traps by the N atoms onto the nanoparticles surface. These energy traps can capture excited electrons, thus avoiding self-quenching and enhancing fluorescence [33].
Fig. 4 shows the comparison of the FTIR spectra of the CDCEL sample with those samples modified with ammonia or PEG1500 N.

The addition of ammonia in the reaction medium generates significant changes in the spectra of carbon nanoparticles (CDCELN), when compared to the unmodified CDCEL sample. In the region between 1550 and 1800 cm−1, typical of CC, CO and CN connections, a clear decrease in the number of bands is observed, suggesting a decrease in the heterogeneity of functional groups, especially carbonyl groups. The peak at 1723 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching of carboxylic groups whereas the signal at 1636 cm−1 is due to stretching vibrations of aromatic CC or CO of amides. The signal at 1100 cm−1 is characteristic of stretching vibrations of C–O and the peak at 1200 cm−1 can be due to C–N stretching [25]. The band at 2267 cm−1 can be attributed to nitriles and eventually to NCO groups. The broad band appearing around 3500-3200 cm−1 for the CDCELN sample corresponds to OH/NH stretching vibrations.

The results obtained from potentiometric titration (Fig. S5 and Table 2 ), show a decrease in the concentration of oxygenated functional groups typically attributed to cyclic esters (lactones). On the other hand, the decrease in the concentration of phenolic groups can be attributed to their reaction with NH3 leading to the formation of amines.

In summary, these results (from FTIR and potentiometric titration) show that in the presence of ammonia, the surface modification of the CDs structure occurs with the reaction of the oxygenated functional groups, ie, esters and anhydrides and eventually carboxylic groups, leading to the formation of nitrogenous functional groups, amines and amides, which undergo dehydration reactions leading to the formation of nitriles, according to Scheme 2 . In general, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen of a nitrile is much less basic than an amine or amide group.

Thus, the presence of ammonia in the reaction medium significantly alters the surface characteristics of the carbon nanoparticles through the formation of nitrogenous functional groups, providing improvements in optical properties, ie, quantum yield of fluorescence. Surface functionalization by different functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and mainly nitrogen groups has been described as effective to improve the optical properties and enhance the QY [34]. The surface functionalization by N-groups (Scheme 2) introduces a new kind of surface state (N-state). The electrons trapped by the newly formed surface states are capable of facilitating a high yield of radiative recombination and inhibit the non-radiative recombination and thus increase the quantum yield [35,36].

In a different and more elaborate strategy to modify the nanoparticles, the CDs were functionalized with the oligomeric diamine terminated PEG1500 N, as shown in Scheme 3 . In this strategy, the carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups can be substituted by amines and (mainly) amide groups.

This strategy was used not only to increase the quantum yield of the carbon nanoparticles but also as an approach to allow a greater interaction of the carbon nanoparticles with metallic ions (through amino groups), which might enable higher sensitivity for the detection of polluting ions.

Fig. 4 shows also the comparison of the FTIR spectra obtained from the CDCEL with the CDCELPEGN sample. After functionalization of the CDCEL sample with PEGN, an increase in the relative intensity of the band centered at 1650 cm−1 is observed, attributed to the formation of amide functional groups on the surface of carbon nanoparticles. There is also a significant increase in the intensity of the band centered at 2890 cm−1, typical of CH stretching, due to the –CH2- groups, as well as the band centered at 1105 cm−1 (C–O–C functional groups), both present in the structure of the PEGN. The region between 1000 and 1300 cm−1 is typical of C–N stretches [25].

Fig. 5 shows transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and HRTEM images obtained from CDCELN (a, b) and CDCELPEGN (c, d) samples. The obtained images showed isolated CDs and also some small aggregates of nanoparticles, mainly for the CDCELN.

The isolated nanoparticles appeared to be monodisperse in size and with a quasi-spherical morphology. From several TEM images, the mean value of the average diameter for the CDCELN sample was found to be in the range of 0.7–2.0 nm with an average size ∼1.2 nm (size distribution histograms with a Gaussian fitting curve shown in Fig. S6a). For the CDCELPEGN sample we have measured a slightly larger diameter, in the range of 1.0–3.0 nm with an average size ∼2.0 nm (Fig. S6b). This result might be due to the outer layer of PEG1500 N oligomers onto the carbon nanoparticles surface.
HRTEM images (Fig. 5b and d) for both CDs show a lattice spacing distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) graphitic plane.

Optical properties are the main characteristics that can be changed after the functionalization of CDs. These properties play a fundamental role in the different possibilities of applications for these nanoparticles. Figs. 6 and 7
show optical properties (UV-VIS absorption and photoluminescence spectra) for both modified carbon nanoparticles, CDCELN and CDCELPEGN. The insets in Figs. 6 and 7 are photographs of the CDs aqueous suspensions illuminated with natural light and with a UV light source. Both samples show a yellow brown color under natural light and a photoluminescence in the blue region of the spectrum considerably stronger than that obtained from the unmodified carbon dot.

The absorption spectra of the modified CDs dispersed in water showed an absorption band around 270 nm for both modified CDs. These bands are due to the π – π* transition of the conjugate CC aromatic band. Both samples also showed a shoulder around 300–330 nm due to n → π* transitions of CO groups.

Photoluminescence spectra for both samples show strong emissions around the blue region of the spectra. Also, it was observed a similar excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior as observed in the unmodified sample, mainly because the surface defects due to the presence of different surface functional groups on the surface of the CDs [3,37].

Measurements of fluorescence intensity as a function of time for CDCELN and CDCELPEGN samples are shown in Figs. S3b and c, respectively. Fig. S3b shows that CDCELN did not suffer any photobleaching behavior whereas Fig. S3c indicated that the CDCELPEGN sample undergo a small photobleaching behavior (with 10% decrease in intensity) after 60 min upon excitation at 340 nm. This behavior may be due to possible destruction of covalent bonds (caused by the excitation light) on the surface of the CDs, partially removing the oligomers containing nitrogen from the nanoparticles surfaces.

The fluorescence quantum yield obtained for the modified carbon dots (Figs. S7 and S8, Supplementary Material) were 6.1% for the CDCELN and 5.4% for CDCELPEGN, showing that both strategies were effective to enhance the QY of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modified CDs as selective probes for different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The modified carbon dots, CDCELN and CDCELPEGN (which showed higher values of quantum yield compared to the unmodified one), were used as probes for different metal ions using the photoluminescence quenching effect.

Firstly, in order to study the selectivity, the quenching effect was investigated for a series of ions (Fe3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Pb2+) and the results obtained for both samples, CDCELN and CDCELPEGN are shown in Fig. 8 a and b, respectively. The measurements of PL intensity were made 2 min after the addition of 25 μL of each metal ion at concentration of 0.01 mol L−1 into the CD suspensions (2.5 mL) at concentration of 0.02 mg mL−1. Fig. 8 shows that the Fe3+ ion exhibited a much more pronounced effect on fluorescence quenching (compared to other metal ions), for both carbon dots. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, the PL intensity of the CDCELN was quenched nearly 90% (Fig. 8a). The sample CDCELPEGN showed even a more significant photoluminescence quenching (Fig. 8b) in the presence of Fe3+. In this case, the emission intensity of the CDs was quenched ∼98% (Fig. 8b).

The fluorescence quenching phenomena of CDs by metallic ions is challenging. Some works reported the induction of aggregation of fluorescent nanoparticles by metallic ions, which can change the efficiency of the absorption/emission of the nanoparticles [38]. On the other hand, it is also described the coordination of ions to the different oxygenated and nitrogen functional groups, which are responsible for the heterogeneity of the emitting sites on the surface of the CDs [39]. In this sense, the results obtained here suggest a great interaction and coordination between ferric ions and different functional groups, such as amino and carboxyl on CDs which work as electron donors to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ (Scheme 4 ).

This great interaction and consequent quenching of the fluorescence is associated not only with the great positive charge of the iron ions, but also with the “optimal” coupling of the electronic levels of the carbon dots with the “d” orbitals of the Fe3+, after the breakdown of degeneracy. Thus, once coordinated on the surface of the CDs, the excited electron is transferred to the electronic level of the Fe3+, which leads to a non-radiative electron-hole recombination, inducing strong fluorescence quenching (Scheme 4).

In addition, the presence of PEG1500 N oligomers linked to the CDs surface might have increased the interactions of the nanoparticles with ferric ions since the fluorescence quenching effect was stronger to the CDCELPEGN sample compared with CDCELN (Fig. 8b).

In order to evaluate the sensitivity of the CDs to the presence of ferric ions (which presented a more pronounced quenching effect), we also studied the PL behavior as a function of Fe3+ ions concentration.

Fig. S9 shows the reduction in CD photoluminescence intensity when different amounts of a 50 μM Fe3+ solution was added into the 0.02 mg mL−1 CD suspensions for both samples, CDCELN and CDCELPEGN. The relation between the reduction of the fluorescence intensity of the CD and the ion concentration is almost linear in the studied concentration range (from 0.3 to 6.1 μM) for both samples.

The fluorescence quenching behavior can be described using the Stern-Volmer equation:
F o
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[
I o n
]

In this equation, Fo is the fluorescence intensity without the presence of the metal ion, F is the intensity measured in the presence of the ion, and KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant. Fig. 9 shows a Stern-Volmer plot for the Fe3+ ion using CDCELN and CDCELPEGN as probes. The Figure shows a good linearity for the concentration range between 0.3 and 6.0 μM.

Using the relation 3δ/S where δ is the standard deviation and S the slope, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated for both samples as being 0.22 μM for CDCELN and 0.15 μM for the CDCELPEGN sample. It means the obtained CDs are not only selective but also sensitive to the presence of low concentrations of ferric ions.

The results described above indicate that the presence of PEG1500 N oligomers and functional amino groups on the surface of the functionalized CDs might have increased the interaction of the nanoparticles with ferric ions, contributing to obtain a considerably low LOD. This behavior can also be seen in Fig. 8, where a more pronounced photoluminescence quenching effect was verified for CDCELPEGN in the presence of Fe3+ compared with the CDCELN sample.

Thus, we believe the presence of PEG1500 N oligomers on the CDs surface might have contributed to the selectivity of the nanoparticles for Fe3+ ions and these modified nanoparticles could be used to probe these ions with a noticeably low limit of detection.
In this work we have prepared different carbon dots from cellulose using hydrothermal carbonization as a green friendly and low cost method.

The CDs were modified by two different methods involving the preparation of carbon nanoparticles in the presence of ammonia or through a reaction of the obtained CDs with PEG1500 N oligomers. Both strategies modified the surface functionalities of the nanoparticles by eliminating oxygenated functional groups and increasing the concentration of nitrogenous functional groups, especially amides and nitriles. As a consequence of the different electronic characteristics of these functional groups, the quantum yields were significantly enhanced for both nanoparticles, increasing these values from ∼1% for the unmodified CD to 5.4 and 6.1% for the nanoparticles modified with PEG1500 N or with ammonia, respectively. Also, the excitation dependent emission behavior was observed for all samples due to different surface functional groups found on the CDs with distinct energy levels.

In addition, the modified nanoparticles were used as sensors for metal ions and the CD sample modified with PEG1500 N oligomers presented a noticeable selectivity for Fe3+ ions and a considerably low limit of detection of 0.15 μM. The interaction between the CDs and the metal ion, which promote the non-radiative electron-hole recombination, is facilitated by the great amount of different functional groups such as amino and carboxyl on the CDs surface.

Finally, this work showed the possibility to prepare CDs from the most abundant polymer found in nature using a low cost method and demonstrated simple strategies to enhance the QY of the carbon nanoparticles to enable their use in different possible applications such as sensors or biomedical area.

Alessandra Pires Passos Zattar: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. João Paulo de Mesquita: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Fabiano Vargas Pereira: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank CNPq (EDT Universal Nº 28/2018) and Fapemig (EDT Universal N° 1/2017) for financial support. Centro de Microscopia-UFMG is also gratefully acknowledged for providing the images.
